name: Cell — Structure and Functions
subject: biology
summary: The cell is life's building block. Learn plant vs animal cells, organelles, and how each part keeps you alive.

crushed.title: Cell = smallest living unit
crushed.text: Every living thing — from bacteria to you — is made of one or more cells. Think LEGO blocks for life.

crushed.title: Plant cells have extras
crushed.text: Cell wall (rigid box), chloroplasts (green solar panels), and a big vacuole (storage tank). Animal cells skip these.

crushed.title: Nucleus = control room
crushed.text: Holds DNA — the instruction manual. It tells the cell what proteins to make and when to divide.

crushed.title: Mitochondria = power plants
crushed.text: Burn glucose + oxygen to release ATP energy. More active cells (muscle) have more mitochondria.

crushed.title: Ribosomes make proteins
crushed.text: Tiny factories that read RNA instructions and stitch amino acids into proteins.

sections.title: Key Organelles at a Glance
sections.intro: Each organelle has one main job. Memorize the job, not every detail.

items.label: Cytoplasm
items.detail: Jelly-like fluid where organelles float and reactions happen

items.label: Cell membrane
items.detail: Thin boundary — controls what enters and leaves

items.label: Endoplasmic reticulum
items.detail: Transport network + rough ER has ribosomes

items.label: Lysosomes
items.detail: Digestive bags — break down waste (suicide bags)

items.label: Cell wall
items.detail: Rigid outer layer — plants & fungi only

flows.title: How a Cell Makes a Protein
flows.description: Follow the journey from DNA instruction to finished protein leaving the cell.

steps.label: Nucleus
steps.description: The gene for a specific protein is transcribed into messenger RNA.

steps.label: mRNA
steps.description: mRNA exits through nuclear pores into the cytoplasm.

steps.label: Ribosome
steps.description: Ribosome reads codons (3-letter codes) and links amino acids.

steps.label: Rough ER
steps.description: New protein enters ER for folding and initial processing.

steps.label: Golgi body
steps.description: Golgi adds tags and packs protein into vesicles.

steps.label: Cell membrane
steps.description: Vesicle fuses with membrane — protein released outside cell.

keyTerms.definition: Structural and functional unit of life
keyTerms.analogy: Smallest LEGO block that can still be 'alive'

keyTerms.definition: Specialized structure inside a cell with a specific function
Cell without a true nucleus (e.g. bacteria)
keyTerms.analogy: Open-plan office — no separate control room

keyTerms.definition: Cell with a membrane-bound nucleus (plants, animals, fungi)
keyTerms.analogy: Office with a locked CEO room (nucleus)

demos.title: Plant Cell — Organelle Tour
demos.description: Zoom from the whole plant into a leaf, then inside one cell to tour each organelle.

steps.label: Whole plant
steps.description: We start with a green plant — every part is built from tiny cells.

steps.label: Zoom into leaf
steps.description: Magnify a leaf — its surface is made of millions of plant cells in a grid.

steps.label: Organelle tour
steps.description: Inside one cell: cell wall, nucleus, chloroplasts, and vacuole light up.

demos.title: From DNA to Protein
demos.description: Follow mRNA leaving the nucleus to a ribosome where a protein is built.

steps.label: Transcription
steps.description: DNA in the nucleus is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA).

steps.label: mRNA travels
steps.description: mRNA exits the nucleus and moves into the cytoplasm.

steps.label: Translation
steps.description: Ribosome reads mRNA and links amino acids into a protein.

chapterSummary.overview: This chapter introduces the cell as the fundamental unit of life. You learn that all living organisms — from bacteria to humans — are built from one or more cells, and that cells contain specialised structures called organelles, each with a distinct function.
keyPoints: Prokaryotic cells (bacteria) lack a true nucleus; eukaryotic cells (plants, animals, fungi) have a membrane-bound nucleus.
Plant cells have a rigid cell wall, chloroplasts for photosynthesis, and a large central vacuole; animal cells lack these.
The nucleus controls cell activities and stores DNA; mitochondria release energy through respiration.
Ribosomes synthesise proteins; ER and Golgi body process and transport them.
The cell membrane is selectively permeable — it controls entry and exit of substances.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Be able to draw and label plant and animal cells. Compare organelles and their functions. Explain why plant cells look box-shaped while animal cells are irregular.
